title: Enter God’s Rest and Draw Near
summary: God’s promise of rest remains open to faith and is grounded in His completed creation work and the continuing Sabbath gift. His living Word exposes the heart, while Jesus, the great and sympathetic High Priest, gives confident access to the throne of grace.
literaryContext: Hebrews 4 continues Psalm 95’s warning by showing that God’s rest reaches beyond Canaan to creation, present trust, Sabbath fellowship, and final inheritance. The warning culminates in accountability before God’s Word, then turns to assurance through the High Priest who has passed through the heavens.
themes: Confidence at the throne of grace

outline.title: The Promise of Rest Remains
outline.summary: Good news must be joined with faith; God’s rest is grounded in His completed creation work and revealed in the seventh day.

outline.title: A Sabbath Rest for God’s People
outline.summary: Canaan did not exhaust the promise. God still speaks today, and a Sabbath-shaped rest remains for those who cease from self-saving works and trust His provision.

outline.title: Diligence before the Living Word
outline.summary: Believers must resist the wilderness pattern because God’s active Word exposes every thought and motive before the One to whom all must give account.

outline.title: Draw Near through the Great High Priest
outline.summary: Jesus has passed through the heavens, understands human weakness without sin, and invites His people to receive timely mercy and grace at God’s throne.

verses.bibleText: Let us therefore fear, lest, a promise being left us of entering into his rest, any of you should seem to come short of it.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The promise left open at the end of Psalm 95 governs this appeal.

Israel’s graves in the wilderness prove that rescue from Egypt did not by itself guarantee entrance into God’s rest.

The congregation must therefore receive the warning with reverent seriousness. “Fear” here is the alertness of faith before a gift that can be neglected, not panic before a reluctant or unpredictable God.

The rest in view is God’s own rest: participation in what He has accomplished and in the inheritance He has promised. No member of the community should merely “seem” to have missed it—language that brings both outward drift and inward reality under examination. A Christian profession, shared worship, or memories of earlier grace cannot replace a present response to God’s voice.

This summons is personal without being individualistic. “Any of you” makes the congregation responsible to notice when someone is falling behind. The preceding chapter has already prescribed mutual encouragement while it is called Today. Weakness, questions, and a repentant fall are occasions for that care; the wilderness danger is a heart gradually settled against trust. Since the promise still stands, the fitting response is watchful confidence: God has prepared the rest, and His people are to keep moving toward it together.

wordNotes.explanation: The noun names a place or state of resting and is repeatedly linked in this passage with God’s own rest. Its range here includes the creation pattern, the promise represented by Canaan, present participation through faith, and the inheritance still ahead; context prevents reducing it to a mere inward feeling.

verses.bibleText: For unto us was the gospel preached, as well as unto them: but the word preached did not profit them, not being mixed with faith in them that heard it.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Two communities heard good news. Israel received the promise of deliverance, God’s presence, and a land in which to live with Him; the church has heard that promise reach its fullness in the Son. Privilege alone did not profit the wilderness generation. The message remained true, yet it met hearers who trusted the visible threat more than the God who had carried them out of Egypt. “Mixed with faith” describes reception rather than supplementation. Faith adds no saving ingredient that God forgot to supply. It welcomes His word as reliable and lets that confidence govern the next step. This is why unbelief and disobedience are paired throughout the passage: refusal to advance toward Canaan exposed the judgment Israel had made about God’s trustworthiness.

The warning reaches beyond ignorance. People may remember sacred history, speak its vocabulary, and live among worshipers while keeping the promise outside the ruling center of life. Hearing becomes profitable as it ripens into reliance. Obedience then appears as faith’s movement, not a payment for grace. The gospel bears fruit where God’s promise is received as news about reality and His voice carries greater weight than fear.

The grammar has a well-known textual difficulty: the message may be described as not united with faith in the hearers, or the hearers as not united by faith with those who listened obediently. Either construction leaves the pastoral point intact. The same announced promise divides a congregation according to response. Caleb and Joshua heard the dangers named by the spies without surrendering God’s word to those dangers; the majority heard the same word and chose fear. Hebrews asks its readers which community of hearers they will join.

verses.bibleText: For we which have believed do enter into rest, as he said, As I have sworn in my wrath, if they shall enter into my rest: although the works were finished from the foundation of the world.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“We which have believed do enter” gives the warning a strong note of assurance. Rest is already being entered wherever people entrust themselves to God, even while its complete enjoyment remains ahead. The oath from Psalm 95 marks the opposite course: persistent unbelief excludes people from the gift they reject. The argument now reaches behind the exodus to creation. God’s works were finished from the foundation of the world, so His rest neither began with Canaan nor depended upon Israel’s success. Canaan became a historical expression of a reality grounded in the Creator’s completed work. Believers participate in that reality by receiving rather than trying to invent the life God gives.

Present rest includes reconciliation, freedom from self-salvation, and fellowship with the One whose provision is sufficient. It also carries an unfinished horizon. Sin and death still trouble creation, and the community still needs warnings against falling away. Faith rests while it travels: it draws its stability from God’s finished work and presses toward the day when His purpose for creation will be fully enjoyed.

Creation also gives rest an objective center. God did not wait for humanity to feel peaceful before declaring His work finished. The believer’s interior peace rests on what God has accomplished outside the self. This guards the passage from turning rest into a mood that vanishes whenever anxiety returns. A troubled person may still be entering by faith, because entrance is defined by reliance on God’s provision rather than by uninterrupted emotional calm.

verses.bibleText: For he spake in a certain place of the seventh day on this wise, And God did rest the seventh day from all his works.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Genesis supplies the foundation for the whole discussion: “God did rest the seventh day from all his works.”

The Creator ceased because the work was complete, ordered, and good. His rest was neither recovery from fatigue nor withdrawal from the world. It was the repose of completion and the enjoyment of what He had made.

The seventh day is therefore part of creation’s structure, not a late ceremonial detail. God blessed and sanctified it before human sin and before Israel existed. Humanity’s first full day was spent inside a work already accomplished. Before the man and woman produced anything, they received life, place, fellowship, and holy time from their Maker. Grace is woven into the weekly rhythm at its beginning.

The Sabbath gives embodied form to that truth. Ordinary work ceases on the seventh day so that human beings may remember the Creator, delight in His gifts, and confess that life is not sustained by endless production. Its holiness rests on God’s act of blessing and setting it apart. Human observance responds to His gift; it does not manufacture the day’s significance.

Hebrews appeals to the seventh day because creation rest still interprets salvation. The author is developing a promise wider than weekly cessation—one that embraces Canaan, present trust, perseverance, and the coming inheritance—but the breadth of that promise does not erase its creation sign. Sabbath and salvation rest illuminate one another. The day teaches people to receive from God before they labor for Him, while the larger promise shows the final fellowship toward which Sabbath points.

Sin disrupted humanity’s enjoyment of God’s rest without canceling His purpose. Each seventh day still bears witness that the Creator completes what He begins and invites His people to live from His provision. Sabbath keeping earns no acceptance; it is a recurring confession that creation and redemption rest in God’s hands.

The author’s introductory formula—“he spake in a certain place”—is no sign of uncertainty about Genesis. Attention rests on the divine Speaker whose voice sounds across the canon rather than on a modern system of citation. Psalm 95 and Genesis 2, though written in different settings, are heard as one coherent testimony: the rest called “my rest” in the psalm is the rest God entered when creation was complete.

That intertext also prevents two opposite reductions. Sabbath cannot be confined to Israel’s possession of Canaan, because its origin precedes Abraham and Sinai. Nor can it be treated as an abstract symbol cut loose from the week, because Genesis identifies a numbered day and records God’s blessing. Hebrews expands the significance of the seventh day without changing which day Genesis sanctifies.

verses.bibleText: And in this place again, If they shall enter into my rest.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The brief repetition of Psalm 95 sets a severe boundary around the promise: “If they shall enter” is the idiom of a sworn refusal. God’s creation rest was real and available, yet the wilderness generation did not share it. Their history exposes no defect in the gift. It records a long resistance to the One who delivered, guided, fed, warned, and invited them. Calling it “my rest” keeps the promise centered on God. Canaan could not be reduced to desirable territory detached from covenant fellowship; nor can salvation be enjoyed while His voice is continually refused. The oath confirms the direction the people chose after repeated appeals.

Yet Hebrews quotes that closed verdict because another Today remains open. Judgment on one generation has not exhausted God’s purpose. Present hearers are not told that mercy is unavailable; they are urged to learn what settled unbelief costs while the invitation can still be welcomed.

verses.bibleText: Seeing therefore it remaineth that some must enter therein, and they to whom it was first preached entered not in because of unbelief:

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Two conclusions follow from Israel’s failure. God’s rest still awaits entrants, and the first hearers missed it “because of unbelief”—a word that can also carry the sense of disobedience. Their response shows why Hebrews moves easily between the two ideas. Distrust became visible when they refused the path God had set before them. The promise survives their rebellion. God will have a people who share His rest, because human resistance cannot empty what He established at creation or overturn His saving purpose. That assurance, however, brings urgency rather than complacency. Covenant membership, past deliverance, and repeated exposure to truth did not carry Israel into the land while the heart opposed God’s voice.

Entrance is wholly provided by grace, yet grace addresses responsible hearers. They cannot create the rest or purchase a place in it; they can refuse to trust the Giver. Hebrews therefore asks its congregation to let former deliverances nourish present obedience and to follow the faithful God where sight alone would hesitate.

The phrase translated “first preached” points back to those who first received the good news of Canaan. Their failure establishes a pattern, not a permanent verdict upon Israel as an ethnic people. Hebrews addresses its own Christian congregation with the same warning, placing every hearer beneath Scripture’s searching voice. The line of division runs through responses to God, not through ancestry.

verses.bibleText: Again, he limiteth a certain day, saying in David, To day, after so long a time; as it is said, To day if ye will hear his voice, harden not your hearts.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Long after the wilderness and after Israel’s settlement in Canaan, God spoke through David and appointed another day: “Today.”

The later psalm proves that Joshua’s generation had not exhausted the promise. Scripture makes God’s earlier word present, placing new hearers before the same choice.

Today is the time in which God’s voice is heard and answered. The word carries tenderness—He still addresses His people—and urgency, because delay changes the listener. Hearts usually harden through accumulated refusals: a correction postponed, a known duty protected from God’s claim, a fear repeatedly granted more authority than His promise.

The appeal is broader than a date or a day of the week. Every genuine encounter with God’s word brings a present summons. The Sabbath supplies a recurring setting for listening and renewed trust, but “Today” names the living moment of response. The warning assumes that the heart can still remain open. Its purpose is to interrupt delay before resistance begins to feel natural.

David’s role matters chronologically and theologically. The psalm comes “after so long a time,” when Israel possessed the land and worshiped within its covenant institutions. Still the Spirit could say “Today.” Rest cannot therefore be equated with a past national achievement. The canonical rereading is already present in Scripture itself: a later inspired voice discloses an uncompleted dimension within an earlier fulfillment.

verses.bibleText: For if Jesus had given them rest, then would he not afterward have spoken of another day.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Joshua gave Israel genuine rest in Canaan: the wandering ended, the tribes received land, and God fulfilled promises made to their ancestors. Hebrews honors that history while observing that Psalm 95 renewed the invitation centuries later. If settlement under Joshua had completed every dimension of God’s rest, Scripture would not have spoken of another day. The land was both fulfillment and sign. It offered a place for covenant life with God, yet it could not cleanse the heart, defeat death, or restore creation. Joshua could lead the people across the Jordan; Jesus leads His people into the fellowship and inheritance to which Canaan pointed.

This pattern keeps present and future together. Believers truly enter rest through faith now, while they await its unbroken fullness. Earlier fulfillments are not discarded; they become evidence that the God who acted in history will complete what remains.

The Greek name for Joshua and Jesus is the same, making the contrast especially vivid to the first hearers. The KJV’s “Jesus” follows that form, though the historical reference is Joshua son of Nun. The wordplay does not diminish Joshua’s faithful service. It distinguishes the provisional leader from the Son who can bring God’s household to glory. Geography alone could never accomplish what the greater Joshua achieves through sacrifice and priesthood.

verses.bibleText: There remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The argument reaches its conclusion with a distinctive word: a “Sabbath rest” still remains for God’s people.

After repeatedly using the ordinary term for rest and explicitly quoting Genesis 2, Hebrews chooses language that evokes Sabbath observance. The creation day has not vanished into an inward metaphor. It gives concrete, recurring expression to the rest God offers.

This Sabbath rest is richer than the bare interruption of labor. The chapter has gathered creation, Canaan, present faith, perseverance, and final inheritance into one movement. Yet that breadth does not cancel the seventh day. God blessed and sanctified it at creation, and its weekly return teaches dependence upon work already completed. Ceasing from ordinary labor confesses that neither life nor salvation is secured by ceaseless human production.

The day is holy because God set it apart. Received as His gift, it becomes time for worship, delight, mercy, restored fellowship, and remembrance of the Creator and Redeemer. Legalism turns observance into a claim upon God; faithful Sabbath keeping answers grace with trust. The cure for misuse is to recover the gift’s purpose, not to discard what God blessed.

“Remains” also opens the future. Sabbath peace is a foretaste of the restored creation, when sin’s disorder and death’s interruption will be gone. Each seventh day stretches from Eden toward that inheritance. God’s people rest because He acted first, live obediently from the life He supplies, and wait for the fellowship their weekly worship anticipates.

The noun is not the usual katapausis heard throughout the paragraph but sabbatismos, a term formed from “Sabbath.” In usage it can denote Sabbath celebration or Sabbath keeping. Its placement after the explicit seventh-day quotation makes the allusion intentional. The author could have repeated his ordinary word for rest; choosing this one lets weekly Sabbath practice and eschatological hope occupy the same sentence.

“For the people of God” also resists the idea that Sabbath belonged only to a discarded ethnic past. Hebrews applies Israel’s Scriptures to a mixed Christian congregation and locates them within the continuing promise. The wording does not suggest that “Today” has replaced the seventh day with another sacred day, nor does the chapter legislate a transfer of holiness. Today concerns the urgency of hearing; the seventh day supplies the creation pattern; sabbatismos names the rest that remains. These strands should be read together rather than used to cancel one another.

wordNotes.explanation: This rare noun denotes Sabbath rest or Sabbath observance. Its use after the quotation of Genesis 2:2 preserves the seventh-day creation setting while the chapter places that weekly gift within the wider promise of sharing God’s rest now and in the coming inheritance.

verses.bibleText: For he that is entered into his rest, he also hath ceased from his own works, as God did from his.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Entering God’s rest means ceasing from one’s works “as God did from his.” The comparison concerns completion, not inactivity. God ended His creative work while continuing to sustain and govern the world. Likewise, believers abandon the project of making themselves acceptable and receive the salvation accomplished through Christ. This cessation changes the source of obedience rather than abolishing it. Works offered to establish a claim before God give way to love, service, and faithfulness flowing from acceptance already given. The seventh-day Sabbath trains this posture in time: ordinary production stops, control is relinquished, and the worshiper acknowledges the sufficiency of Creator and Redeemer.

The rest is present but unfinished. Christians still labor, resist sin, and journey toward the kingdom. They do so from communion rather than toward self-made security. Faith rests in God’s achievement and therefore becomes free for the work love requires.

The pronoun may refer generally to anyone who enters or, as some have proposed, particularly to Christ completing His saving work. The immediate comparison with God and the following plural exhortation favor a general statement about entrants, though the rest believers enjoy is inseparable from Christ’s accomplishment. The wording alone should not carry more than the paragraph requires. Its settled emphasis is cessation grounded in a work God has brought to completion.

verses.bibleText: Let us labour therefore to enter into that rest, lest any man fall after the same example of unbelief.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“Let us labour” is the deliberate effort of perseverance. Rest would contradict effort only if it meant indifference. Hebrews describes active reliance on God—reliance that must resist the wilderness pattern of complaint, self-protection, and refusal.

Israel did not fall because the promised land had to be earned. The people fell when they would not trust the promise enough to follow. Christian diligence takes the opposite form: continued listening, prompt return after correction, mutual encouragement, and attachment to Christ when retreat appears safer. None of this adds merit to grace. It keeps receiving the way, strength, and inheritance grace supplies.

The warning names a pattern that can be imitated, so it can also be interrupted. A stumble need not become a settled course. God calls weary people to the throne of grace in the verses ahead, where mercy supplies the perseverance He commands. The KJV’s “labour” translates a verb of earnestness or diligence, not ordinary employment. The irony is deliberate: people must be earnest about entering rest. Such earnestness includes learning from Israel’s narrated failure instead of treating biblical history as someone else’s cautionary tale. The written “example” gives present communities a recognizable sequence—privilege, complaint, delay, hardening—so they can seek help while the sequence can still be broken.

verses.bibleText: For the word of God is quick, and powerful, and sharper than any twoedged sword, piercing even to the dividing asunder of soul and spirit, and of the joints and marrow, and is a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The warning carries divine force because God’s word is “quick”—living—and active.

In context, that word includes the promise and summons of Psalm 95. What addressed Israel’s wilderness failure now addresses the church. Scripture is no museum echo; the living God continues to speak through it.

The two-edged sword pictures penetration. Soul and spirit, joints and marrow are paired images of depths beyond ordinary human inspection, not a technical map dividing the person into detachable substances. God’s speech reaches beneath conduct and reputation to the hidden springs of action.

There it is “a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart.” The adjective translated “discerner” conveys skilled judgment. God’s word tests the reasoning and purposes that people themselves confuse: fear may wear the clothing of prudence, pride may mimic conviction, and sincere faith may tremble beneath visible weakness. Its verdict is exact because the Speaker knows the whole person.

This searching activity is medicinal as well as judicial. The same voice that uncovers unbelief still promises rest and soon directs exposed sinners to a sympathetic High Priest. Scripture should therefore be allowed to question the reader before it is used to control someone else. Its cutting clears away concealment so that repentance, faith, and healing may begin in truth.

Its reach to “soul and spirit” also fits Hebrews’ whole-person anthropology. The paired terms intensify penetration; they do not portray a naturally immortal conscious soul detachable from the body. “Joints and marrow” work the same way, taking the image into what lies sealed within the frame. The piling up of pairs says that no boundary constructed by human analysis can stop God’s examination.

Because the word judges motives, interpreters should approach others with humility. A correct action may arise from mixed reasons, and an apparent hesitation may conceal honest wrestling. Human counsel remains fallible; the divine assessment is final. Scripture’s searching role calls communities to truthful discernment while denying them permission to claim God’s exhaustive knowledge of another heart.

wordNotes.explanation: The adjective describes one able to evaluate or judge. Applied to God’s word, it conveys searching assessment of the heart’s reasoning and intentions, not merely the communication of information.

verses.bibleText: Neither is there any creature that is not manifest in his sight: but all things are naked and opened unto the eyes of him with whom we have to do.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Nothing created is concealed from the God whose word searches the heart. Motives hidden from neighbors—and partly hidden from ourselves—remain “naked and opened” before His eyes. Covenant identity, religious vocabulary, and an admired reputation cannot cover the inward refusal that ruined the wilderness generation. Such exposure makes judgment morally serious and also perfectly just. God never works from incomplete evidence. He knows each act together with its intention, opportunity, wound, pressure, and chosen loyalty. “Him with whom we have to do” is the One to whom every life must finally give account.

The paragraph does not strand the exposed sinner beneath that gaze. Its next sentence announces the great High Priest. The God who sees without illusion has provided the representative through whom sinners may approach without disguise. Grace becomes precious precisely here: it meets people in the full truth rather than in a version of themselves polished for inspection.

The movement is from accountability to access. Concealment offers no safety, but confession brings the known sinner toward a throne of grace. Jesus understands the weakness God sees and stands in God’s presence for His people. Honest approach is therefore wiser than flight.

The unusual verb behind “opened” may evoke a neck laid bare for inspection or an opponent whose head is forced back. Either image communicates helpless exposure, but the exact picture is uncertain. Hebrews needs no gruesome reconstruction to make its point. Every created thing stands immediately visible to the Creator.

This knowledge also answers the fear that suffering or faithfulness can be misread forever. God sees hidden unbelief, but He equally sees hidden fidelity, coercion, trauma, opportunity, and the burdens under which choices were made. His omniscience is threatening to concealment and consoling to those whom human judgment has misunderstood. Accountability before Him is exact because His sight is complete.

verses.bibleText: Seeing then that we have a great high priest, that is passed into the heavens, Jesus the Son of God, let us hold fast our profession.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“Seeing then” turns the searching judgment of verses 12–13 toward assurance. Christians do not stand exposed without a representative: they have a great High Priest who has “passed into the heavens.” Jesus’ resurrection and ascension brought Him into God’s presence, where He ministers on behalf of His people. His sacrifice and priesthood must be distinguished without being separated. The offering of Himself was completed once for all and can never be repeated. The risen Christ nevertheless remains active as covenant Mediator, interceding, granting access, and administering the benefits secured at the cross. His heavenly ministry rests on a finished sacrifice.

“Jesus” keeps His solidarity with humanity in view; “the Son of God” declares the greatness that surpasses every earthly priest. The One who shared flesh and blood now occupies the heavenly position promised to the royal Son. Because He has reached the destination ahead of His household, believers are told to hold fast their confession. Perseverance rests less on the intensity of their grip than on the identity and faithfulness of the Priest they possess.

“Great” distinguishes Jesus from the succession of Israel’s high priests, and “passed through the heavens” answers the earthly pattern in which a priest passed through sanctuary spaces toward God’s symbolic presence. Hebrews is speaking of a real heavenly ministry without reducing heaven to a larger copy of an earthly building. The earthly sanctuary was the patterned shadow; Christ serves in the true sphere of God’s presence.

Holding fast is communal as well as private. “Our profession” is the confession believers share: Jesus the Son of God is the sufficient Priest. Public pressure can make that confession costly, which is why Hebrews repeatedly joins doctrine to endurance. The church steadies its speech by looking to where Jesus is and what He is doing.

verses.bibleText: For we have not an high priest which cannot be touched with the feeling of our infirmities; but was in all points tempted like as we are, yet without sin.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The great High Priest is capable of fellow feeling with human weakness. Hunger, exhaustion, grief, rejection, pain, loneliness, and the pressure of approaching death were realities of Jesus’ incarnate life. Exaltation has not made Him a stranger to any of them. Tempted “in all points like as we are” describes the full range of human testing rather than every individual circumstance. Jesus faced pressure to meet legitimate needs independently of the Father, evade suffering, grasp approval, and leave the path of obedient trust. The testing was genuine; His faithfulness was complete.

“Without sin” guards His unique fitness to save. Weakness and temptation are not themselves guilt, and Jesus never consented to evil. His sinlessness deepens rather than thins His sympathy: He endured the pressure to its full extent instead of escaping through surrender. Believers may therefore bring temptation into His presence honestly. They meet neither indulgence nor contempt, but the understanding and effective help of One who knows the struggle and has conquered.

His solidarity also establishes the moral character of God’s rule. The throne is occupied neither by indifference to embodied pain nor by ignorance of the pressures that accompany it. In the Son, divine compassion has entered those pressures personally. This makes the coming invitation to bold approach more than a legal permission; it is an approach to Someone who knows how temptation feels from the inside.

The phrase offers no excuse for sin, yet it removes shame from the fact of being tempted. Shame often drives struggle underground until isolation strengthens it. Hebrews instead brings weakness into priestly fellowship, where honesty can receive timely grace.

verses.bibleText: Let us therefore come boldly unto the throne of grace, that we may obtain mercy, and find grace to help in time of need.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The proper movement in weakness is toward God: “come boldly unto the throne of grace.” Boldness means open, assured access rather than casual entitlement. Jesus’ sacrifice and living priesthood, not the worshiper’s record, make such freedom possible. God’s throne remains the seat of holy rule and judgment, yet through Christ it is known as the place where mercy and grace are given. Mercy meets guilt and misery. Grace supplies strength, wisdom, endurance, and whatever help faithfulness requires. The phrase translated “in time of need” suggests help well-timed for the actual moment, not a remote promise stored beyond the crisis.

No one is asked to master weakness before approaching. The invitation follows the declaration that God already sees everything and that Jesus sympathizes with human frailty. Prayer can therefore be candid. Temptation, shame, confusion, and exhaustion belong in the presence of the High Priest. The safest response to exposure is nearness to the One who has opened the way.

The plural exhortation again imagines a worshiping community. Access through Christ belongs to all His people, not to an elite class repeating the old priestly distance. Earthly sanctuary restrictions taught the seriousness of holiness; the great High Priest now makes genuine approach possible without making holiness trivial.

The two purposes—obtaining mercy and finding grace—match the congregation’s situation. They need pardon for failure and power for the next faithful act. “Find” suggests discovering in God exactly what the crisis requires. The throne is therefore the practical answer to the command to persevere: divine help meets human need at the point where endurance is actually tested.
